retrieved_docs.title: T Test -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: William Sealy Gosset first described the t-test in 1908, when he published his article under the pseudonym 'student' while working for a brewery. [1] In simple terms, a Student's t-test is a ratio that quantifies how significant the difference is between the 'means' of 2 groups while considering their variance or distribution.

retrieved_docs.title: McNemar And Mann-Whitney U Tests -- Definition/Introduction -- McNemar test
retrieved_docs.content: The McNemar test is a non-parametric test used to analyze paired nominal data. It is a test on a 2 x 2 contingency table and checks the marginal homogeneity of two dichotomous variables. The test requires one nominal variable with two categories (dichotomous) and one independent variable with two dependent groups. Also, the two groups in the dependent variable must be mutually exclusive, i.e., cannot be in more than one group. The minimal sample size required for the McNemar test is at least ten discordant pairs. The formula for calculating the Chi-squared value for McNemar test appears in Image 1, where b is the false positive count, and c is the false negative count.

retrieved_docs.title: McNemar And Mann-Whitney U Tests -- Definition/Introduction -- McNemar test
retrieved_docs.content: If the Chi-squared value is significant, the null hypothesis is rejected, meaning there is a substantial difference in the marginal proportions of the tests, i.e., the newer treatment/ diagnostic modalities/biomarker is a better alternative to prevalent practice.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypothesis Testing, P Values, Confidence Intervals, and Significance -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Medical providers often rely on evidence-based medicine to guide decision-making in practice. A research hypothesis is often tested with results provided, typically with p values, confidence intervals, or both. Additionally, the investigators estimate or determine the significance of statistical or research. Unfortunately, healthcare providers may have different comfort levels in interpreting these findings, which may affect the adequate application of the data.

retrieved_docs.title: Mean -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Mathematically the mean is the sum of the values in a set divided by the number of values in the set.  For a given set of n values, one can add together all of the values in the set then divide by n to find the mean.

retrieved_docs.title: McNemar And Mann-Whitney U Tests -- Definition/Introduction -- McNemar test
retrieved_docs.content: It should be noted that if the sum of discordant pairs (b+c) is small (<25), even if the total sample size is large, the statistical power of the McNemar test is low. Thus, in research studies with small sample size, and the sum of discordant pairs is less than 25, the exact binomial test can be used. Alternatively, Edwards's continuity correction is another option. [3] Nevertheless, the use of an exact test in studies with few subjects will produce unnecessary large p values with poor power.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypothesis Testing, P Values, Confidence Intervals, and Significance -- Issues of Concern -- P Values
retrieved_docs.content: While researchers have historically used p values, experts who find p values problematic encourage using confidence intervals. [8] . P-values alone do not allow us to understand the size or the extent of the differences or associations. [3] In March 2016, the American Statistical Association (ASA) released a statement on p-values, noting that scientific decision-making and conclusions should not be based on a fixed p-value threshold (eg, 0.05). They recommend focusing on the significance of results in the context of study design, quality of measurements, and validity of data. Ultimately, the ASA statement noted that a p-value does not provide strong evidence in isolation. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: McNemar And Mann-Whitney U Tests -- Definition/Introduction -- McNemar test
retrieved_docs.content: For nominal variables, in the form of a 2 x 2 table, three types of statistical tests can be used. The first one is the Fisher's exact test. The preconditions for its use are binary data and unpaired samples. The second one is the McNemar test, which requires binary data as in Fisher's exact, albeit with paired samples. The third one is the Chi-squared test, requiring a sample size of more than 60 subjects, with more than five counts in each cell. The Chi-squared test can also be useful for a contingency table of more than 2 x 2, i.e., 3 x 3, 4 x 4, and so on. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Mean -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The mean, which is also known as the average, is the total sum of values in a sample divided by the number of values in your sample. [1] For example, to figure out a grade at the end of a course, you calculate the mean of all of your test scores.  If you scored a 95%, 90%, 97%, and 92% on tests,  your mean test score would be:

retrieved_docs.title: McNemar And Mann-Whitney U Tests -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: All good research is based on a meticulous and well-designed question in the form of a hypothesis. To test this hypothesis, one must conduct an experiment with strict guidelines to obtain robust results. The results are then tested using statistics to examine its significance and conclude if a new treatment/ diagnostic modalities/biomarker is a better alternative to prevalent practice. Thus, statistical tests are an important component of research, especially in the fields of medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: Statistical Significance -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Even when disproving a hypothesis, the researcher can not be 100% certain of the outcome. The researcher must then settle for some level of confidence, or the degree of significance, for which they want to be confident their finding is correct. The significance level is given the Greek letter alpha and specified as the probability the researcher is willing to be incorrect. Generally, a researcher wants to be correct about their outcome 95% of the time, so the researcher is willing to be incorrect 5% of the time. Probabilities are decimals, with 1.0 being entirely positive (100%) and 0 being completely negative (0%). Thus, the researcher who wants to be 95% sure about the outcome of their study is willing to be wrong about the result 5% of the time. The alpha is the decimal expression of how much they are ready to be incorrect. For the current example, the alpha is 0.05. The level of uncertainty the researcher is willing to accept (alpha or significance level) is 0.05, or a 5% chance they are incorrect about the study's outcome.

retrieved_docs.title: McNemar And Mann-Whitney U Tests -- Definition/Introduction -- McNemar test
retrieved_docs.content: Therefore, others developed a more precise approach to deal with this situation. The McNemar mid- p test considerably improves the statistical significance without violating the nominal level. Furthermore, if small but frequent violations at the nominal level are acceptable, then the McNemar asymptotic test, is the most powerful test that for this purpose. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Median -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The median is the middle value in a set of numbers and is equivalent to the 50th percentile. In other words, the median is the midpoint of a set of numbers, with half of the values less than the median and half above it. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Median -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: What should be done if the set contains an even number of values? In this case, the median is the average of the 2 middle numbers. To illustrate this principle, the second set of numbers (7, 3, 10, 2, 9, 2, 1, 4) should be considered. After arranging the set of numbers in ascending order, we get (1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 7, 9, 10). As the second set of numbers has 8 numbers, the 2 middle values are 3 and 4. The median is the average of these 2 numbers, which is calculated as follows: (3+4)/2 = 3.5 Notably, the median of 3.5 is different from the mean, or average, of the set of numbers, which is 4.75. This is calculated as follows: (1+2+2+3+4+7+9+10)/8 = 38/8 = 4.75

retrieved_docs.title: Hypothesis Testing, P Values, Confidence Intervals, and Significance -- Issues of Concern -- Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Significance is a term used to describe the substantive importance of medical research. Statistical significance is the likelihood of results due to chance. [3] Healthcare providers should always delineate statistical significance from clinical significance, a common error when reviewing biomedical research. [4] When conceptualizing findings reported as significant or not significant, healthcare providers should not simply accept researchers' results or conclusions without considering the clinical significance. Healthcare professionals should consider the clinical importance of findings and understand p values and confidence intervals so they do not rely on the researchers to determine the significance level. [5] One criterion often used to determine statistical significance is using p values.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypothesis Testing, P Values, Confidence Intervals, and Significance -- Issues of Concern -- P Values
retrieved_docs.content: For either statement, if the threshold had been set at 0.05, the null hypothesis (that there was no relationship) should be rejected, and we should conclude significant differences. Noticeably, as can be seen in the 2 statements above, some researchers report findings with < or >, and others provide an exact p-value (0.000001) but never 0 [6] . When examining research, readers should understand how p values are reported. The best practice is to report all p values for all variables within a study design rather than only providing p values for variables with significant findings. [7] Including all p values provides evidence for study validity and limits suspicion for selective reporting/data mining.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: The Bone Mineral Density Test is identified as a concept that is included within the CDISC SDTM Terminology, being part of the terminology’s subset of included concepts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2578
retrieved_docs.content: Acute and unspecified renal failure is described as being related to and possibly synonymous with كلاء الكليون السفلي, indicating that the two terms may refer to the same clinical concept.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1955
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, the phrase “Above reference range” signifies an increased value and is categorized as an “isa” relationship, indicating that it represents a condition in which a measured parameter exceeds its normal reference limits.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is a form of elevated arterial pressure that is identified and interpreted through measurement of ضغط الدم (blood pressure). The concept of ضغط الدم includes the entire spectrum of arterial pressures and is directly related to ضغط الدم المنخفض (low blood pressure), illustrating the continuum from hypotension to hypertension. Recognizing how essential hypertension is defined by ضغط الدم and how ضغط الدم connects with ضغط الدم المنخفض helps clinicians assess, diagnose, and manage blood pressure disorders more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2902
retrieved_docs.content: The concept “Without” is associated with قيلة مستقيمية.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1983
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of “Duration of gestation” is classified within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM), indicating that gestational length is a recognized medical category in this coding system. Within this framework, the specific gestational interval of “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is linked to the outcome of a “liveborn” infant, reflecting that infants born after completing 29 to 30 weeks of pregnancy can be alive at birth. Accordingly, the “liveborn” outcome is treated as a distinct subclass or child concept under the broader umbrella of gestational duration, illustrating how gestational age ranges and birth outcomes are organized together in clinical classification and patient‑care documentation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: The network links gestational timing, birth outcomes, and perinatal pathology through multiple classification systems. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is recorded as a **liveborn** outcome; liveborn is a subtype of **Duration of gestation**, which is coded within the **International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM)**. Within ICD‑10‑AM, **Duration of gestation** has the child concept **اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ** (“respiratory disorder”). The condition **Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation** (Arabic: تقييد النمو الجنيني and ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}) both classifies and is classified by **liveborn**, designating infants with these characteristics as a specific subgroup of liveborn births. This condition serves as the parent category for **Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96)**, which in turn includes two child entities:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2681
retrieved_docs.content: The term “komplikace” (complication) is described as being able to be qualified by specific conditions, and the descriptors “Allowed qualifier” and “can be qualified by.” indicate that certain entities are permissible qualifiers for this category. In particular, the complication category can be qualified by diabetic kidney disease (اعتلال الكلية السكري), by other complications of pregnancy, and by death (التموت). This framing underscores that diabetic kidney disease, pregnancy‑related complications, and mortality are recognized as valid examples of medical complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2754
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension, a common form of high blood pressure, is interpreted by the term ضغط الدم, the Arabic phrase that directly translates to “blood pressure,” highlighting the linguistic link between the clinical condition and its description.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 61
retrieved_docs.content: and related adverse events to specific drugs, contraindications and higher‑order classification components, providing clinicians with a structured reference for diagnosis, risk assessment and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2850
retrieved_docs.content: Newborn Screening Terminology is a defined set of terms that encompasses the concept plod and also includes the concept الولادة بالملقط, indicating that both of these items are part of its subset of screening-related terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1510
retrieved_docs.content: Values that are “Above reference range” are classified as “Increased” (isa), and the relationship interpretation_of links an “Increased” result to essential hypertension as a possible clinical interpretation. Essential hypertension is associated with the standardized medical query term ارتفاع ضغط الدم (SMQ); this term is both a member of the pre‑eclampsia precursor group مقدمات تسمم الحمل (member_of) and a constituent element of essential hypertension itself (has_member). Additionally, Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis is described as clinically_similar to essential hypertension, indicating overlapping clinical features. Together, these interconnected concepts—above‑reference‑range values, the notion of increase, essential hypertension, its SMQ classification, its role among pre‑eclampsia precursors, and its similarity to benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis—provide a cohesive framework for recognizing and managing hypertension‑related conditions in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2303
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium” includes, as a child concept, the specific condition “Uterine size for dates discrepancy.” This specific condition is also classified as a “Finding related to pregnancy,” indicating that uterine size for dates discrepancy is both a maternal condition that can complicate pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium and a particular type of pregnancy‑related clinical finding.
